(2019)](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2019.37)."}, {"url": "https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s41748-024-00491-0", "title": "Permafrost Thawing and Estimates of Vulnerable Carbon in ...", "summary": "Abstract The degradation of permafrost in the Northern Hemisphere is expected to persist and potentially worsen as the climate continues to warm. Thawing permafrost results in the decomposition of organic matter frozen in the ground, which stores large amounts of soil organic carbon (SOC), leading to carbon being emitted into the atmosphere in the form of carbon dioxide and methane. This process could potentially contribute to positive feedback between global climate change and permafrost carbon emissions.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Using data from the latest generation of climate models (CMIP6), this paper explores the challenges involved in assessing the annual active layer thickness (ALT), defined as the maximum annual thaw depth of permafrost, and estimated carbon released u", "CMIP6 models project ALT changes of 0.1\u20130.3 m per degree rise in local temperature, resulting in an average deepening of approx. 1.2\u20132.1 m in the northern high latitudes under different scenarios.", "With increasing temperatures, permafrost thawing starts in Southern Siberia, Northern Canada, and Alaska, progressively extending towards the North Pole by the end of the century under high emissions scenarios (SSP5-8.5).", "Using projections of ALT changes and vertically resolved SOC data, we estimate the ensemble mean of decomposable carbon stocks in thawed permafrost to be approximately 115 GtC (gigatons of carbon in the form of CO2 and CH4) under SSP1-2.6, 180 GtC un", "Similar content being viewed by others 1 Introduction Permafrost, defined as ground that remains frozen (with a temperature less than 0 \u00b0C) for an extended period, typically for at least two years, covers nearly a quarter of the Northern Hemisphere l"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Using data from the latest generation of climate models (CMIP6), this paper explores the challenges involved in assessin"}, {"url": "https://www.ipcc.ch/srocc/chapter/chapter-2/", "title": "Chapter 2: High Mountain Areas", "summary": "ES Executive Summary The cryosphere (including, snow, glaciers, permafrost, lake and river ice) is an integral element of high mountain regions, which are home to roughly 10% of the global population. Widespread cryosphere changes affect physical, biological and human systems in the mountains and surrounding lowlands, with impacts evident even in the ocean. Building on the IPCC\u2019s 5th Assessment Report (AR5), this chapter assesses new evidence on observed recent and projected changes in the mountain cryosphere as well as associated impacts, risks and adaptation measures related to natural and human systems.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["ES Executive Summary The cryosphere (including, snow, glaciers, permafrost, lake and river ice) is an integral element of high mountain regions, which are home to roughly 10% of the global population.", "Snow cover duration has declined in nearly all regions, especially at lower elevations, on average by 5 days per decade, with a likely2 range from 0\u201310 days per decade.", "Mass change of glaciers in all mountain regions (excluding the Canadian and Russian Arctic, Svalbard, Greenland and Antarctica) was very likely -490 \u00b1 100 kg m-2 yr-1 (-123 \u00b1 24 Gt yr-1) in 2006\u20132015.", "Regionally averaged mass budgets were likely most negative (less than -850 kg m-2 yr-1) in the southern Andes, Caucasus and the European Alps/Pyrenees, and least negative in High Mountain Asia (-150 \u00b1 110 kg m-2 yr-1) but variations within regions ar", "Between 3.6\u20135.2 million km2 are underlain by permafrost in the eleven high mountain regions covered in this chapter corresponding to 27\u201329% of the global permafrost area (medium confidence)."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: ES Executive Summary The cryosphere (including, snow, glaciers, permafrost, lake and river ice) is an integral element o"}, {"url": "https://tntcat.iiasa.ac.at/RcpDb", "title": "RCP Database", "summary": "RCP Database (version 2.0): The RCP database aims at documenting the emissions, concentrations, and land-cover change projections of the so-called \"Representative Concentration Pathways\" (RCPs). Information about the RCPs and the scenario development process for the IPCC AR5 can be found in the IPCC Expert Meeting Report on New Scenarios and Moss et al. (2010). For a draft work plan summarizing the data exchange between the Integrated Assessment and Climate Modeling community see also the \"Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) Draft Handshake\".", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["RCP Database (version 2.0): The RCP database aims at documenting the emissions, concentrations, and land-cover change projections of the so-called \"Representative Concentration Pathways\" (RCPs).", "Information about the RCPs and the scenario development process for the IPCC AR5 can be found in the IPCC Expert Meeting Report on New Scenarios and Moss et al. (2010).", "The final RCPs have been documented in a Special Issue of Climatic Change that was published in November 2011 (Climatic Change, Volume 109, Issue 1-2).", "The overview paper (van Vuuren et al, 2011) of the Special Issue summarizes the main achievements.", "Version 2.0 of the database includes harmonized and consolidated data for three of the four RCPs."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: RCP Database (version 2.0): The RCP database aims at documenting the emissions, concentrations, and land-cover change pr"}]}
